coredata.dc:title: Red emissive carbon dots Self-Cascading antioxidant nanozymes combine with anti-miRNA-155 for accurate monitoring and ameliorating rheumatoid arthritis
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Youcong
Huang, Jinkun
Xing, Xiaotong
Liu, Huiyu
Zhou, Zijia
Dong, Haifeng
coredata.dc:description: Accurate monitoring and effective antioxidant treatment strategies are highly desirable in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) amelioration. Herein, cerium (Ce) and selenium (Se) co-doped carbon dots (CS-CDs) integrated superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like and glutathione peroxidase (GPx)-like self-cascading catalytic activity are engineered. In this self-cascading nanozymes system, Ce active center catalyzes ·O2 − to H2O2 by mimicking SOD, and the adjacent Se component sequentially decomposes the H2O2 by mimicking GPx, achieving enhanced catalytic and superior reactive oxygen species (ROS)-scavenging activities. CS-CDs further load cyanine-5 (Cy5)-labeled anti-miRNA-155 to realize macrophage subphenotype identification via miRNA-155 ratiometric imaging for RA accurate monitoring, and regulate the polarization of pro-inflammatory macrophages for synergistic RA amelioration. Comprehensive in vitro and in vivo experiments validate the advanced RA monitoring and therapy performance of the self-cascading nanozymes. This work not only offers a rationally designed self-cascading nanozyme with simple structures and intriguing ROS-scavenging capacity, but also opens up a strategy for accurate RA monitoring and synergistic amelioration.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the production and use of CS-CDs/Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 (CS-CDs@155). (A) Synthesis of CS-CDs via the hydrothermal reaction between citric acid, p-phenylenediamine, cerium nitrate, sodium selenite, and deionized water and then combined with Cy5-anti-miRNA-155. The Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 could be adsorbed on the surface of CS-CDs through π–π stacking interaction leading to Cy5-FL quenching. (B) After CS-CDs@155 were uptake by M1 macrophages, Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 hybridized with miRNA-155 to form double-stranded hybrids and then away from CS-CDs resulting in Cy5-FL recovery. CS-CDs@155 not only eliminates excessive ROS and prevents inflammation for synergistic RA amelioration but also accurately monitors the progression of RA.

Characterization of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs. (A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CS-CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs. (C) Excitation spectrum (Ex) and fluorescence (FL) emission spectra (Em) of CS-CDs; the inset image shows the CS-CDs solution under UV light irradiation. (D) FL emission spectra of CS-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (E) Zeta potentials of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs. (F) FT-IR spectrum and (G) XPS spectrum of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs. (H, I) High-resolution spectra of Ce 3d (H) and Se 3d (I) in CS-CDs.

In vitro antioxidant efficacy. (A) Schematic illustrating the scavenging of ROS by CS-CDs. (B) ·OH-scavenging activities of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs (20 μg mL−1), determined by the salicylic acid method. (C) ·OH-scavenging efficiency of CS-CDs at different CS-CDs concentrations. (D) ·O2 −-scavenging activities of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs (5 μg mL−1), determined by SOD assay kit with WST-8 (Shanghai, China). (E) ·O2 − scavenging efficiency of CS-CDs at different CS-CDs concentrations. (F) The GPx-like activity of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs (20 μg mL−1), determined by total glutathione peroxidase assay kit with NADPH (Shanghai, China). (G) In vitro antioxidant activity of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs (20 μg mL−1) determined by ABTS free radical scavenging assays. (H, I) CLSM images of RAW264.7 cells (H) and FLS (I) following LPS stimulation and the CDs-incubation treatments. Green FL intensity indicated intracellular ROS levels.

CS-CDs protect cells from oxidative stress and eliminate the adverse effects of high ROS. (A) Effects of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs on the invasion of LPS-stimulated FLS. Cells were stained with crystal violet. (B) Effects of undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs on the actin cytoskeleton of LPS-stimulated FLS. Actin stress fibers (green) and nuclei (blue) were stained with Actin-Tracker Green and DAPI, respectively. The control group represented non-activated FLS. (C,D) FLS (C) and RAW264.7 cells (D) without and with different treatments that TNF-α-only, and preincubated with undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, and CS-CDs and then exposed to TNF-α (100 ng/mL). The apoptosis rate was determined by flow cytometry. Apoptotic cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC/PI.

The combined effects of CS-CDs and Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 on M1 macrophage. (A) The CD68 (green) and CD206 (red) immunofluorescence staining images of LPS-treated RAW264.7 cells with or without pretreatment of Lip@155, CS-CDs, and CS-CDs@155. (B, C) ELISA detection results of TNF-α, IL-6, IL-10, and TGF-β1 in LPS-stimulated RAW264.7 cells. (D) The protein levels of iNOS and Arg-1 in RAW264.7 cells with different treatments.

In vitro performance of the CS-CDs@155-D for miRNA-155 ratiometric imaging. (A) The FL quenching effect of CS-CDs on 155-D. (B) FL spectra of the 155-D in PBS (pH = 7.4, 10 mM) and in CS-CD solution (100 μg mL−1) with and without pre-incubating target (T) miRNA-155 (25 nM). (C) FL spectra of CS-CDs@155-D (100 μg mL−1) incubating with miRNA-155 (250 nM) at different indication times. (D) Representative CS-CDs@155-D (100 μg mL−1) FL spectra in the presence of target with different concentrations for one hour. (E) FL spectra of (a) CS-CDs (100 μg mL−1), (b) CS-CDs@155-D (100 μg mL−1) without target and with (c-l) target ranging from 6.25 nM to 500 nM undergoing excitation at 490 nm. (F) Relationship and linear relationship (inset) between FCy5 /FCS-CDs for different target concentrations. (G) CLSM images of CS-CDs@155-D-treated RAW264.7 cells polarized into different macrophage phenotypes. (H) Quantitative data of FL intensity of (G).

In vivo synergistic therapeutic effect evaluation. (A) Overall experimental timeline of immunization and treatments for therapeutic efficacy evaluation in AIA mice. (B) The FL images of normal or AIA mice taken at 6 h post injection of CS-CDs@155. (C) Ratios of right ankle joint width to left ankle joint width (WR/WL) in untreated/treated mice. I = control group, II–V = PBS, Lip@155, CS-CDs, and CS-CDs@155 groups, respectively. (D) Representative micro-CT images of the right ankle joints of untreated/treated mice, acquired at day 45. (E-G) Quantitative analysis of BMD (E), BV/TV (F), and Tb.Sp (G) in the ankle joints of untreated/treated mice. (H) H&E staining of the synovial membranes of untreated/treated mice. Scale bars: 50 µm. (I) Safranin-O staining images of the right ankle joints of untreated/treated mice. Scale bars: 100 µm.

head.ce:title: Red emissive carbon dots Self-Cascading antioxidant nanozymes combine with anti-miRNA-155 for accurate monitoring and ameliorating rheumatoid arthritis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marshall Laboratory of Biomedical Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory for Nano-Biosensing Technology, School of Biomedical Engineering, Shenzhen University Medical School, Shenzhen University, Guangdong 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory for Nano-Biosensing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Marshall Laboratory of Biomedical Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory for Nano-Biosensing Technology, School of Biomedical Engineering, Shenzhen University Medical School, Shenzhen University, Guangdong 518060, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate monitoring and effective antioxidant treatment strategies are highly desirable in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) amelioration. Herein, cerium (Ce) and selenium (Se) co-doped carbon dots (CS-CDs) integrated superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like and glutathione peroxidase (GPx)-like self-cascading catalytic activity are engineered. In this self-cascading nanozymes system, Ce active center catalyzes ·O2 − to H2O2 by mimicking SOD, and the adjacent Se component sequentially decomposes the H2O2 by mimicking GPx, achieving enhanced catalytic and superior reactive oxygen species (ROS)-scavenging activities. CS-CDs further load cyanine-5 (Cy5)-labeled anti-miRNA-155 to realize macrophage subphenotype identification via miRNA-155 ratiometric imaging for RA accurate monitoring, and regulate the polarization of pro-inflammatory macrophages for synergistic RA amelioration. Comprehensive in vitro and in vivo experiments validate the advanced RA monitoring and therapy performance of the self-cascading nanozymes. This work not only offers a rationally designed self-cascading nanozyme with simple structures and intriguing ROS-scavenging capacity, but also opens up a strategy for accurate RA monitoring and synergistic amelioration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by persistent synovitis and the progressive destruction of the cartilage [1–3]. The vicious circle of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and inflammation mediated by macrophages plays a critical role in the progression of RA [4,5]. Macrophages are key components of innate immunity [6], consisting mainly of two subgroups, M1 and M2, which initiate and resolve inflammation, respectively [7]. Macrophages in RA joints are mainly M1 phenotype [8], secreting pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-6 (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) to promote ROS production. In turn, ROS can promote the polarization of M1 macrophages, resulting in secrete more pro-inflammatory cytokines and the induction of inflammation [9,10]. Thus, these factors form a positive feedback loop between inflammation and ROS, constituting a vicious cycle that ultimately induces RA progression [11]. Therefore, eliminating excessive ROS and silencing the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, which in turn regulate M1 macrophage to M2 polarization holds great promise for RA treatment.

Within the natural antioxidant system, superoxide dismutase (SOD) acts as the first line of defense, converting superoxide anion radical (·O2 −) into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [12]. The resultant H2O2 molecules are then decomposed to H2O by catalase (CAT) or glutathione peroxidase (GPx) [13,14]. Continuous scavenging of ·O2 − and H2O2 via cooperation between SOD/CAT or SOD/GPx yields satisfactory antioxidant effects [15–17], however, the localization of these natural antioxidant enzymes in different subcellular compartments means that additional transport steps are required for the generation and decomposition of H2O2 [18]. In addition, these enzymes may become depleted or inactivated under pathological conditions [19]. Therefore, considerable therapeutic benefits may be achieved by the design of artificial enzymes with ROS cascade scavenging activity that are capable of mimicking the intracellular antioxidant enzyme defense system.

In recent years, various cascade nanozymes are developed by integrating or assembling different nanozymes into a single unit [20,21]. However, complicated fabrication processes, instability and potential side effects are involved [22]. Versatile nanozymes with self-cascading capacity, simple structures and superior antioxidant activity are highly desirable. Cerium (Ce)-based nanozymes exhibit outstanding SOD-like activities, attributed to the reversible conversion of Ce4+ and Ce3+ [23]. Nonetheless, the SOD-like activities of Ce-based nanozymes are impaired in a high-H2O2 oxidizing environment because of the oxidation of Ce3+ to Ce4+ by H2O2 [24]. Addressing this challenge requires a strategy to integrate Ce-based nanozymes with an additional material exhibiting GPx-like activities. The prompt decomposition of H2O2 by a GPx-mimic would maintain the stability of Ce3+/Ce4+ and SOD activity. The efficiency Ce-based of SOD/GPx self-cascading nanozymes for scavenging ·O2 − and H2O2 may even be expected to surpass the physiological antioxidant enzyme system as the transport of H2O2 is not required. However, very few works have been explored to construct Ce-based SOD/GPX nanozymes for RA antioxidant treatment.

Apart from scavenging excessive ROS, polarizing macrophages toward the M2 phenotype to resolve inflammation is promising in RA amelioration [25]. Interestingly, M1/M2 polarization of macrophages is associated with microRNAs (miRNAs) expression [26]. Emerging data suggest that miRNA-155, which is highly expressed in M1 macrophages while lowly expressed in M2 macrophages, can drive the polarization of M2 macrophages to an M1 phenotype via multiple pathways [27]. The key target genes of miRNA-155 is suppressor of cytokine signaling 1 (SOCS1), a negative regulator of signal transducers and activators of transcription-1 (STAT1), which mediates signaling from pro-inflammatory cytokines [28]. MiRNA-155 directly targets SOCS1 to decrease its expression, thereby increasing proinflammatory cytokine [29]. Inhibition of miRNA-155 using antisense oligonucleotides has been shown to facilitate M1 to M2 switching [30]. Considering the vital role of macrophages in RA, the development of a versatile platform for monitoring miRNA-155 expression in macrophages and regulating its expression is beneficial to RA monitoring and amelioration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis, and characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
ROS-scavenging nanozymes show significant therapeutic benefits in the treatment of RA [31]. We selected heteroatom-doped CDs to construct self-cascade antioxidant nanozymes to continuously eliminate ·O2 − and H2O2, the major pathogenic ROS in RA. Before investigating their antioxidant activity, we conducted a thorough evaluation of their properties, as outlined below. First, the undoped CDs was prepared according to previous reports with modification [32]. The optimal molar ratio of citrate acid and p-phenylenediamine was determined as 1:1 owing to stronger and red-shift FL emission compared to other groups after the hydrothermal reaction ( Fig. S1
, Supporting Information). Next, we prepared Ce-doped CDs (Ce-CDs) and Se-doped CDs (Se-CDs) using Ce(NO3)·6H2O and Na2SeO3 as the Ce and Se sources, respectively, and citrate acid and p-phenylenediamine (1:1) as the carbon sources. The doping of Ce resulted in negligible FL changes in Ce-CDs. The doping of Se in CDs led to obvious FL changes in the Se-CDs (Fig. S2), and the products with red FL emission produced by a 1:1 M ratio of Na2SeO3 and mixture containing citric acid/p-phenylenediamine (1:1) was used for in our following experiments due to its better penetration. Finally, the CS-CDs were prepared by introducing Ce sources during the Se-CDs preparation.

Analysis of the structure of the prepared CDs by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that all CDs were well-dispersed, with consistent particle sizes of 2–3 nm (Fig. S3 and Fig. 2 A). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 2A) confirmed that the lattice spacing of the CS-CDs was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphene, this graphene structure is beneficial to electron transfer and thus increases antioxidant activity [33]. UV/Vis absorption spectra were used to evaluate the optical properties of the CDs preparations (Fig. 2B). Absorption peaks close to 377 nm were detected for aqueous solutions of undoped CDs and Ce-CDs, while two peaks of 363 nm and 490 nm were detected for both Se-CDs and CS-CDs. These absorption peaks at 490 nm corresponded to low-energy regions and were mainly caused by the surface states of the samples, implying that the introduction of Se had a marked effect on the surface state of the CDs. The FL spectra of CS-CDs were similar to Se-CDs, with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 490 nm and 630 nm, respectively (Fig. 2C). In addition, no obvious shifts in the emission peaks of the four prepared CDs were observed at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S4 and Fig. 2D). These results indicated that the emission wavelength of these CDs was independent of the excitation wavelength. The Ce-CDs exhibited higher zeta potential than undoped CDs, suggesting the successful introduction of Ce ions (Fig. 2E), while the lower zeta potential of Se-CDs compared to the undoped CDs confirmed the negatively charged Se-O group introduction. We then analyzed the functional groups and elemental composition of the CDs using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectra demonstrated that similar hydrophilic groups were existed in all four CDs, including N/O-H (3400 ∼ 3300 cm−1) and –COOH (1716 cm−1) groups, indicative of good water solubility (Fig. 2F). Peaks at 1612 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C = C and C = N, respectively, indicating the existence of unsaturated double bonds in the CDs. Notably, Ce-CDs exhibited an enhanced absorption peak at 1716 cm−1 attributed to the pre-coordination of –COOH with Ce3+ before the hydrothermal reaction, resulting in increased C = O content in the carbon core. The full-scan XPS spectra confirmed that Ce-CDs were mainly composed of C, N, O, and Ce, while Se-CDs were mainly composed of C, N, O, and Se (Fig. 2G). The high-resolution XPS analysis of the C 1 s peaks showed that the intensity (289.0 eV) was increased in Ce-CDs, relative to undoped CDs (Fig. S5), which was consistent with the FT-IR analysis results. And the peak intensity at 284–288 eV was increased in Se-CDs compared to the undoped CDs, indicating C-Se bonds were formed. Moreover, the Ce 3d deconvolution XPS spectrum (Fig. S6) indicated Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 spin-orbits, demonstrating the coexistence of CeIII and CeIV in Ce-CDs, while the Se 3d deconvolution XPS spectrum further confirmed that C-Se units were presented in Se-CDs (Fig. S7). Importantly, Ce with mixed valence (Fig. 2H) and C-Se units (Fig. 2I) were observed to occur simultaneously in CS-CDs, validating the potential SOD-like and GPx-like activities of CS-CDs [34,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro SOD-like and GPx-like activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Having confirmed the successful doped of Ce or Se in the CDs, we wanted to compare their antioxidant properties. Therefore, we evaluated the ROS scavenging activity of the four CDs preparations, and hydroxy radicals (·OH), ·O2 −, and H2O2 as three representative ROS were selected for analysis (Fig. 3 A) [36,37]. First, we used the salicylic acid method to evaluate the ability of CDs to scavenge ·OH. We observed direct elimination of this ROS by all four CDs (Fig. 3B), which was attributed to their inherent unsaturated structure [38]. The Se-CDs and CS-CDs performed somewhat better than either undoped CDs or Ce-CDs, which may be explained by the formation of adducts between Se atoms and ·OH radicals, thus further promoting ·OH quenching. The ·OH scavenging ability of the CS-CDs was progressively enhanced as the concentration was increased, with approximately 78 % of ·OH radicals eliminated in the presence of 100 μg mL−1 CS-CDs (Fig. 3C). Next, to verify the ability of the CS-CDs to cascade scavenging ROS, we investigated ·O2 − scavenging performance. Ce-CDs and CS-CDs exhibited enhanced abilities to inhibit ·O2 − radicals, compared with undoped CDs and Se-CDs, indicating that the Ce component mediated SOD-like activity (Fig. 3D). Again, ·O2 − scavenging activity was progressively enhanced as CS-CDs concentration increased (Fig. 3E). We also evaluated GPx-like properties of the CS-CDs, which are responsible for the decomposition of H2O2. For this analysis, we used the classical glutathione reductase coupling method, in which the progress of the catalytic reaction is monitored by measuring absorbance changes in the coenzyme nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate at 340 nm (Fig. 3F). The results showed that absorbance values at 10 min were only decreased by 15 % in the presence of undoped CDs or Ce-CDs, while in contrast, the addition of Se-CDs or CS-CDs to the reaction system caused a 70 % decrease in absorbance intensity. These findings confirmed GPx-like activity in the CS-CDs and showed that it was mainly attributed to the Se component. Finally, the total antioxidant capacity of the CDs preparations was evaluated using 2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) free radical scavenging assays [39,40]. CS-CDs exhibited higher ABTS radical scavenging ability and stronger antioxidant activity than the other CDs (Fig. 3G). Overall, our experimental results showed that CS-CDs exhibited both SOD-like and GPx-like activities, mainly mediated by the Ce and Se components, respectively, providing evidence for the cascade scavenging of ·O2 − and H2O2 by CS-CDs.

After demonstrating the ability of CS-CDs to cascade scavenging ROS, we went on to explore intracellular antioxidant activity, first assessing the cytotoxicity of the four CDs preparations. The results of a standard CCK-8 assay showed that the survival rates of mouse mononuclear macrophages (RAW264.7) and fibroblast like synoviocytes (FLS) remained above 90 % after 24 h incubation with 0–100 μg mL−1 of the four CDs (Fig. S8). In addition, apoptosis was almost undetectable in the control and treatment groups by flow cytometry (Fig. S9), demonstrating that the CDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity at 100 μg mL−1. Therefore, we selected 100 μg mL−1 as the maximum concentration of CS-CDs to be used in cell experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular ROS-scavenging activity of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before investigating the intracellular antioxidant ability of the CS-CDs, we used confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) to analyze the distribution of CS-CDs in FLS. Detection of intracellular fluorescent signals demonstrated that CS-CDs could enter cells and were localized within the cytoplasm (Fig. S10A), and some CS-CDs were distributed in mitochondria and lysosomes, respectively (Fig. S10B and C), indicating that the intracellular location of CS-CDs was non-specific, which may facilitate their ROS elimination behavior in cells [41]. Subsequently, we stimulated FLS and RAW264.7 cells with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) to establish the RA-related cell model and monitored intracellular ROS levels using a fluorogenic redox probe, 2‘,7‘-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), which oxidized by ROS and emitted enhanced green FL signal. Comparison of LPS-treated RAW264.7 cells and FLS and untreated controls revealed higher levels of green FL in the LPS-treated cells, indicating elevated intracellular ROS levels following stimulation with LPS (Fig. 3H and I). By contrast, FL was suppressed following incubation of the cells with CDs, with CS-CDs displaying the most prominent ROS-scavenging ability; these findings were further confirmed by flow cytometric analyses (Fig. S11 and Fig. S12). These findings demonstrated the potent antioxidant capacity of CS-CDs, which can likely be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the SOD-like and GPx–like activities. Our results show that CS-CDs are promising candidates for use as ROS scavengers, with the potential to ameliorate the conditions of high oxidative stress that characterize the RA microenvironment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds protect cells from high ROS-induced adverse effects
ce:section.ce:para: Next, given the potent ROS scavenging capacity of CS-CDs, we evaluated the effect of CS-CDs on the invasiveness of LPS-activated FLS. Under normal conditions, FLS are the most abundant cell type in the healthy human synovium, functioning to nourish the cartilage and maintain and shape the synovial extracellular matrix by secreting hyaluronic acid and lubricin [42]. ROS regulates the invasion of FLS via multiple pathways, which plays a key role in the progression of RA [43]. Crystal violet staining showed that CS-CDs prevented the invasiveness of FLS more effectively than undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, or Se-CDs (Fig. 4 A). It was resulted from the greater ROS-scavenging capacity of the CS-CDs (compared with the other CDs) and the resulting enhanced suppression of ROS-induced effects. Moreover, as cytoskeletal reorganization is known to contribute to FLS invasion, we also examined the effects of CDs on actin stress fiber formation in LPS-treated FLS. Staining of LPS-treated FLS and untreated controls with Actin-Tracker Green showed that LPS increased actin stress fiber formation. The intensity of actin stress fibers in LPS-activated FLS was reduced by CDs treatments, especially, the CS-CDs treatment induced the greatest decrease (Fig. 4B).

As a further measure of the cellular effects of the experimental CDs under RA-like conditions, we exposed FLS and RAW264.7 cells to the pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α as an in vitro model of RA. Analysis of apoptosis by flow cytometry showed that the rate of apoptosis in the TNF-α-only group was 37.72 %, while decreased to 30.77 %, 24.19 %, 22.84 %,10.21 % in TNF-α-treated FLS cells that had been pre-treated with undoped CDs, Ce-CDs, Se-CDs, CS-CDs, respectively (Fig. 4C). Similarly, compared with the TNF-α-only group, the rate of apoptosis was decreased in TNF-α-treated RAW264.7 cells that had been pre-incubated with CDs, particularly CS-CDs (Fig. 4D). We further conducted live/dead double staining on the same cell lines to determine the inhibitory effects of the CDs on TNF-α-induced cell death. The levels of red FL signals were substantially decreased in cells pre-treated with CS-CDs, in comparison with TNF-α-treated FLS and RAW264.7 cells, indicating lower numbers of dead cells in this treatment group (Fig. S13). These experimental results showed that CS-CDs effectively attenuate TNF-α-induced oxidative stress, thus improving cell survival, which has potential implications for RA treatment.

Next, we further investigated the protective effects of CS-CDs on other cells, using transwell co-culture systems designed to mimic the RA inflammatory microenvironment (Fig. S14). In this co-culture system, abundant ROS and pro-inflammatory cytokines produced by activated RAW264.7 cells diffused into the lower chamber, inducing oxidative stress and apoptosis in the L929 cells. The results of caspase-3 staining to detect apoptosis demonstrated that CS-CDs treatment effectively suppressed the induction of apoptosis in L929 cells by activated macrophages (Fig. S15). These findings were confirmed by flow cytometric analysis data, which showed that 61.12 % of L929 cells treated with PBS (pH = 7.4,10 mM) were undergoing apoptosis, while an apoptotic rate of 13.06 % was observed in CS-CDs-treated cells (Fig. S16). Apoptotic cell numbers in the other treatment groups showed smaller reductions of 54.51 % to 40.95 %. Notably, the highest rate of cell survival was observed in the CS-CDs-treated group, demonstrating the ability of CS-CDs to protect normal cells from oxidative stress caused by differing stimuli and eliminate the adverse effects of high ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cs-cds combined with Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 to promote M1-to-M2 switching
ce:section.ce:para: Considering miRNA-155 is highly expressed in M1 macrophages and has an important role in promoting the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines [27], we wanted to evaluate the therapeutic potential of combining ROS scavenging with miRNA-155 regulation. As the above results demonstrated that CS-CDs exhibited the most potent antioxidant properties of the four CDs preparations analyzed, we explored the combined effects of CS-CDs and Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 on cytokine production by pro-inflammatory macrophages. First, we verified that Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 could hybridize with miRNA-155 to form double-stranded hybrids (Lane 3) via complementary base pairing interactions by 10 % polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (Fig. S17). Next, we investigated the feasibility of positively charged CS-CDs to carry negatively charged Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 to realize synergistic therapeutic effects. Agarose gel electrophoresis result revealed that Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 completely retarded migration when pre-incubation with CS-CDs (Fig. S18), suggesting that they were fully combined at an appropriate ratio.

Next, we further investigated the performance of CS-CDs@155. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) result demonstrated that the hydrated particle size of CS-CDs@155 was near 11 nm (Fig. S19). The Raman spectrum depicted two peaks at 1378 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 (Fig. S20), which were assigned to the D- and G-bands of graphene, respectively [44]. The absolute FL quantum yields of CS-CDs and CS-CDs@155 were both calculated to be 7 %. These findings indicate that Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 has little effect on the FL signal of CS-CDs. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was used to detect the ·O2 − and ·OH in Xanthine/Xanthine oxidase or Fenton reaction by using 5,5′-dimethylpyrroline-1-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trapping agent [45–48]. The signal intensity of ·O2 − and ·OH was decreased after the addition of CS-CDs@155, indicating that it can scavenge ·O2 − and ·OH directly (Fig. S21A and B). We further used methyl violet degradation methods to evaluate the effect of CS-CDs@155 inhibiting ·OH and found that ·OH was scavenged by CS-CDs@155 in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. S22). These findings indicate that Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 did not damage the antioxidant performance of CS-CDs. Low CAT-mimicking activities were observed under the same concentration of CS-CDs@155 as the inhibiting ·OH activity test, suggesting that CS-CDs@155 could not eliminate H2O2 rapidly and directly (Fig. S23). We went to explore the intracellular antioxidant activity of CS-CDs@155 and CCK-8 assays were carried out. The results showed that CS-CDs@155 had no obvious cytotoxicity under 100 μg mL−1 (Fig. S24). To mimic the ROS-abundant condition, we used H2O2 to cause ROS-mediated apoptosis. The cell viability gradually decreased as the H2O2 concentration increased (Fig. S25A). In the following experiments, 400 μM H2O2 was used to induce apoptosis in RAW264.7 cells and to test the ability of CS-CDs@155 to reduce oxidative damage. The cell viability improved with increasing concentrations of CS-CDs@155 (Fig. S25B), confirming the cytoprotective effect of CS-CDs@155.

We further evaluated the effect of CS-CDs@155 on M1 macrophage. LPS-activated RAW264.7 cells were treated with Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 only (transfected using liposomes, Lip@155), CS-CDs only, or CS-CDs@155, and surface biomarkers such as CD86 (M1 marker) and CD206 (M2 marker) were detected by immunofluorescent staining (Fig. 5 A). The intensity of green FL in LPS-activated RAW264.7 cells was reduced by Lip@155, CS-CDs, and CS-CDs@155 pretreatments, especially, the CS-CDs@155 pretreatment induced the greatest decrease. In addition, CS-CDs@155 greatest enhanced the intensity of red FL. And cytokine levels in RAW264.7 cells were analyzed by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The results showed that the CS-CDs@155 group can down-regulated the expression of pro-inflammatory TNF-α and IL-6 more effectively than Lip@155 and CS-CDs (Fig. 5B). This finding can be explained by the synergistic effects of CS-CDs@155, which targets both the pro-inflammatory regulator miRNA-155 and ROS. Moreover, the CS-CDs@155-pretreated group also exhibited the highest levels of anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10 and TGF-β1), indicative of an M1-to-M2 phenotype switch in these cells (Fig. 5C). The results were further proved by analysis of induced nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) and arginase-1(Arg-1) expression via western blot (Fig. 5D and Fig. S26). Taken together, these results indicate that CS-CDs@155 exerts synergistic effects on inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine production and ROS-scavenging levels that trigger the repolarization of M1 macrophages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cs-cds for the ratiometric FL sensing of miRNA-155
ce:section.ce:para: Accurate monitoring of inflammatory processes is of great value for early diagnosis and disease intervention [49], therefore, in situ imaging of miRNA in pro-inflammatory M1 macrophage is highly necessary. Anti-miRNA-155 inhibits the function of miRNA-155 by forming a double strand with miRNA-155 and then degrading it. Considering the difference in adsorption capacity of CDs to single/double-stranded nucleotides [50], as well as the intrinsic FL of the CDs, we attempted to employ CS-CDs@155 as a ratiometric imaging platform for miRNA-155 and macrophage phenotype identification. In our approach, the Cy5-labeled synthetic DNA to mimic anti-miRNA-155 (155-D) was first prepared, which was assembled with CS-CDs to obtain the CS-CDs@155-D probe. Before testing the feasibility of the probe for miRNA-155 imaging in macrophages, we first evaluated the FL-quenching effect of CS-CDs with different concentrations to 155-D. The FL intensity of Cy5 at 670 nm gradually decreased with increasing CS-CDs concentration, reaching a minimum value at 100 μg mL−1 of CS-CDs, and the quenching efficiency was calculated to be 90 % (Fig. 6 A). The adsorbing capacity of 155-D by CS-CDs was calculated by the FL standard curve method, which showed an adsorption density of 0.22 nmol mg−1 (Fig. S27). However, there was a negligible decrease in the FL intensity of Cy5 when 155-D was pre-incubated with the target miRNA-155 before being mixed with the CS-CDs (Fig. 6B), indicating that CS-CDs did not quench the FL signal of double-stranded hybrids due to the weak interaction between the duplex structure and CS-CDs [51].

The introduction of miRNA-155 to the CS-CDs@155-D mixture restored the fluorescent probe signal in less than one hour (Fig. 6C). As expected, we also found that the FL intensity of the probe increased in a concentration-dependent manner with the addition of the miRNA-155 (Fig. 6D), which confirmed that the miRNA-155 hybridized with the 155-D to make it disassociation from the CS-CDs. In contrast, the FL signal of the CS-CDs was unchanged (Fig. 6E). These findings prompted us to use CS-CDs as an internal stable reference signal for ratiometric accurate FL analysis. Indeed, we observed a linear relationship between the FCy5 /FCS-CDs ratio and target concentration ranging from 25 to 175 nM (Fig. 6F). Further evaluation of the performance of CS-CDs@155-D (Fig. S28) showed that the introduction of other miRNA targets resulted in minimal changes in FL intensity, indicating the high selectivity of this system.

Next, we investigated the performance of the CS-CDs@155-D for intracellular miRNA-155 imaging and macrophage phenotype monitoring. First, we induced RAW264.7 cells to polarize into M1 or M2 macrophages using LPS or IL-4, respectively, and incubated the cells with CS-CDs@155-D for 6 h. As revealed by CLSM (Fig. 6G), the intensity of red FL signal in M1 macrophages was higher than in M2 macrophages. Quantitative analysis of CLSM images showed that the mean FL intensity of Cy5 in the LPS-treated group was 5.3-fold higher than that of the IL-4-treated group, using the FL of CS-CDs as the reference signal (Fig. 6H). These results demonstrated the successful binding of the 155-D to intracellular miRNA-155, confirming that CS-CDs@155-D could indeed be used for discriminating M1 from M2 by miRNA-155 ratiometric imaging. These findings are expected to enable the visualization of the biological function of macrophages, differentiating pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory macrophages to monitor RA progression.

Motivated by the above in vitro results and to test the therapeutic effects of CS-CDs@155 in vivo, we used the murine AIA model, which shares important pathological features of human RA, including chronic synovitis and cartilage destruction [52]. This model was established by injecting complete Freund's adjuvant into the right ankle joint of BALB/c mice at the appropriate time (Fig. 7 A). Fifteen days after the first immunization, the right ankle joint of those mice led to distinct swelling (Fig. S29). Next, the CS-CDs@155 was injected in situ into mice pretreated with or without complete Freund's adjuvant, and FL images were collected in the following 6 h. The FL intensity of CS-CDs was approximately equal in normal and AIA mice while the FL intensity of Cy5 in AIA mice was higher than in normal mice (Fig. 7B). These results suggested that macrophages with highly expressed miRNA-155 were accumulated in the inflamed ankle joint and the AIA model mice were successfully established. These findings indicate the great potential for CS-CDs@155 to accurately RA monitoring by identifying macrophage phenotypes via miRNA-155 ratiometric imaging.

Next, the 24 AIA mice were randomly divided into four groups (N = 6), which then received intra-articular injections of PBS (group II), Lip@155 (group III), CS-CDs (group IV), or CS-CDs@155 (group V); a control group of mice did not receive complete Freund's adjuvant or any other treatment (group I). There were no obvious body weight fluctuations in any mice during either the immunization or drug administration periods (Fig. S30). To evaluate the effects of the various treatments, we assessed the degree of joint swelling in the experimental animals by calculating the ratio of the width of the right inflamed ankle to the left normal ankle (WR/WL), in which it eliminated the influence of natural joint growth on the overall results. While Lip@155 treatment led to a slight alleviation of ankle joint swelling, compared with the PBS-treatment group, CS-CDs treatment reduced ankle joint swelling by 45.6 %, and CS-CDs@155 reduced it by 82.3 %, bringing the WR/WL ratio down to almost the normal level (Fig. 7C and Fig. S31). In addition, the FL intensity of Cy5 in CDs@155-treated mice was reduced to near-normal levels (Fig. S32), suggesting M1 macrophages have switched. These results confirmed the in vitro findings, again indicating the superior therapeutic efficacy of CS-CDs@155 compared with the other treatments, which was resulted from the synergistic effect of self-cascading ROS-scavenging and inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Moreover, consistent with these findings, analysis of IL-6 and TNF-α in serum after 45 days by ELISA kits revealed increased levels of these pro-inflammatory cytokines in AIA mice, compared with normal mice, and remarkably decreased levels in CS-CDs@155-treated mice in comparison with other groups (Fig. S33).

To verify the therapeutic effects of CS-CDs@155, we further carried out microcomputed tomography (micro-CT) and histological examination to analyze the main features of RA, namely cartilage destruction and inflammatory cell infiltration. Micro-CT images revealed rough bone surface and bone erosion in the PBS treatment group, which were largely alleviated in the CS-CDs@155 group (Fig. 7D) but not the CS-CDs or Lip@155 groups. In addition, quantitative micro-CT analysis of the ankle joints of AIA mice showed that bone mineral density (BMD) and bone volume/total volume (BT/TV) were decreased (Fig. 7E and F) and trabecular separation (Tb.Sp) was increased (Fig. 7G), indicating severe bone loss in the joint due to arthritis. In contrast, these indicators showed recovery to near-normal levels in the CS-CDs@155 group. Next, synovitis was assessed by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of the right ankle joint (Fig. 7H). While AIA mice exhibited severe synovial hyperplasia and immune cell infiltration, these pathological phenomena were suppressed after treatment with CS-CDs@155, indicating that the inflammation had been resolved. Moreover, safranin-O staining of the ankle joint revealed severe destruction of cartilage in AIA mice (Fig. 7I). By contrast, CS-CDs@155-treated mice exhibited clearer cartilage boundaries than the other treatment groups and were more comparable to the control group in this respect. Overall, these findings indicate the potent bone-and joint-protective effects of CS-CDs@155 in RA.

Finally, we evaluated the in vivo biosafety of these treatment systems. For this purpose, we randomly divided 24 healthy BALB/c mice into 4 groups (N = 6) and administered PBS, Lip@155, CS-CDs, or CS-CDs@155 by intra-articular injection. Importantly, routine blood tests and indicators of hepatic and renal function were all within the normal range (Fig. S34). Moreover, no obvious pathological changes were observed in H&E-stained sections of major organs (Fig. S35), indicating the high biocompatibility of our newly-developed treatment systems.

In summary, we proposed an ingenious strategy for assembling CS-CDs with Intriguing self-cascading antioxidant ability, with Cy5-anti-miRNA-155 to obtain multifunctional theranostic nanoplatforms. Based on this strategy, CS-CDs@155 can achieve RA imaging monitoring and synergistic effect of ROS-scavenging and anti-inflammatory for RA amelioration. The assembly of CS-CDs@155 is achieved in a simple step while its performance is versatile. Compared to conventional SOD/CAT nanosystem for ROS-scavenging, firstly, CS-CDs is executing SOD/GPx activity, which avoids the generation of highly toxic ·OH potentially. For example, although MnO2 and Pt were found to possess CAT-like activities [21,53], they may catalyze H2O2 to produce ·OH under acidic conditions. Secondly, compared to traditional antioxidant nanosystem reprogramming macrophage phenotype by eliminating excessive ROS or pro-inflammatory cytokines [54,55], CS-CDs@155 can also regulate and image the pro-inflammatory mediator miRNA-155, which is conducive to monitoring the disease progression and the precise treatment of RA. Finally, in contrast to conventional FL nanoprobes [56], CS-CDs are used as a quencher simultaneously as the internal stable reference signal, which improves the reproducibility and reliability of the detection result.

Taken together, we expected this work might provide new perspectives for designing self-cascading nanozyme to mimic the intracellular antioxidant enzyme defense system and would inspire the construction of novel nanozyme-based cooperative systems for monitoring and ameliorating macrophage polarization imbalance-associated diseases in the future.
The detailed experimental processes are available in the Supporting Information.
